qa_pairs.question: What is the CCDB on Alliance clusters?
qa_pairs.answer: The CCDB is the portal to your Alliance account, where you can find information on your project roles, allocated resources, and usage statistics.

qa_pairs.question: What can users do on the Alliance CCDB portal?
qa_pairs.answer: Users can register, manage personal information and roles, apply for the Resource Allocation Competition (RAC), and manage RAP information and membership.

qa_pairs.question: What is a CCI and why is it important for Alliance users?
qa_pairs.answer: A CCI is a unique personal and national identifier (e.g., abc-123) created when you register with the Alliance CCDB. It serves as your primary identifier within the Alliance system.

qa_pairs.question: Who is eligible to register for an Alliance CCI?
qa_pairs.answer: To register for an Alliance CCI, you must be a faculty member at a Canadian institution registered with the CCDB, or be sponsored by such a faculty member. Sponsored individuals include graduate students and research staff.

qa_pairs.question: What does a "role" signify in the Alliance CCDB system, and why is it important to maintain?
qa_pairs.answer: A role identifies your position (e.g., faculty, graduate student), institution, and sponsor. It is required to access computational resources, and a new role must be applied for when your institution, sponsor, or position changes to ensure accurate reporting to funding agencies.

qa_pairs.question: What is a CCRI and how is it related to a CCI in the Alliance system?
qa_pairs.answer: A CCRI is the unique identifier for a specific role (e.g., abc-123-01). Since one person (one CCI) can have multiple roles over time, one CCI may be associated with several CCRIs. Every job on a national cluster is attributed to a CCRI.

qa_pairs.question: What is a Resource Allocation Project (RAP) in the context of Alliance computational resources?
qa_pairs.answer: A RAP (Resource Allocation Project) is how Principal Investigators (PIs) gain access to national computational resources. Each RAP, identified by a RAPI and group name, represents a group of researchers to whom resources are allocated.

qa_pairs.question: What are the two main types of RAPs in the Alliance system, and what is the difference between them?
qa_pairs.answer: The two main types are Default RAPs, automatically created for PIs for opportunistic use, and RAC RAPs, created when a PI receives an award through the Resource Allocation Competition for dedicated allocations.

qa_pairs.question: What is a group name in the Alliance system, and how is it used?
qa_pairs.answer: A group name is an alias for a Research Allocation Project Identifier (RAPI), used because it's often easier to remember. It follows specific conventions (e.g., def-profname, rrg-profname-xx) and functions as a POSIX group name in LDAP to define users authorized to submit jobs and share files.

qa_pairs.question: Who automatically gets access to a PI's Default RAP in the Alliance system?
qa_pairs.answer: All of a PI's activated sponsored user roles are automatically members of their Default RAP. This membership cannot be modified, though a PI can deactivate any role they sponsor.

qa_pairs.question: How does membership work for a RAC RAP with HPC resources compared to one with Cloud resources in the Alliance system?
qa_pairs.answer: For RAC RAPs with HPC resources, all of the PI's sponsored users, associated Co-PIs, and their sponsored users are automatically added. For RAC RAPs with Cloud resources, only the PI is initially added as a member.

qa_pairs.question: What are the permission levels for managing membership within a RAC RAP in the Alliance CCDB?
qa_pairs.answer: There are three levels: Owner (the PI, who manages managers and members), Manager (delegated by owner/manager, can make membership changes on CCDB and use allocations), and Member (can use allocations but cannot modify RAP membership).

qa_pairs.question: If a user previously had an Alliance account but their position or sponsor has changed, should they register for a new CCI?
qa_pairs.answer: No, each person can only have one Alliance account (one CCI). If your position or sponsor has changed, you should log in with your existing account and apply for a new role (which will generate a new CCRI) instead of creating a new CCI.

qa_pairs.question: What are the general categories of roles that can be selected when applying for an Alliance account?
qa_pairs.answer: Roles fall into two main types: sponsor roles (for faculty members, often PIs) and sponsored roles. Sponsored roles include students (undergraduate, masters, doctoral), researchers affiliated with the PI's institution, non-research staff, and external collaborators from different institutions.

qa_pairs.question: What is the approval process after submitting an application for an Alliance account?
qa_pairs.answer: You will first receive an email to confirm your address. If you are a PI, your application will be approved by Federation staff. If you are a sponsored user, your application must first be confirmed by your identified sponsor PI before being approved by Federation staff.

qa_pairs.question: Whom should a user contact for help with questions not covered in the Alliance CCDB FAQ?
qa_pairs.answer: For any questions not covered in the FAQ, users should send an email to accounts@tech.alliancecan.ca.
